ontology.foodex2_description: Sponge cake — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for Swiss roll (rolled sponge cake with cream filling); no dedicated Swiss roll code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Swiss roll entry; A00GJ (sponge cake) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Swiss roll as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Swiss roll is one slice of approximately 60 g, representing roughly one-sixth of a standard small roll.

nutrient_source.title: Swiss Roll — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F02010018 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: Sponge cake, plain — GI value 66 (analogous estimate for Swiss roll); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Swiss roll exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest approved analogous food is plain sponge cake (GI 66, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Swiss roll is a rolled sponge cake with cream filling; the cream filling does not substantially alter the GI of the sponge base. A conservative estimate of GI 65 is applied. Glycemic load per 60 g serving = (65 × 35.6 × 0.60) / 100 = 13.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (20.2 g/100 g) with low fibre (1.5 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (9.5 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Swiss roll as a regular snack because its high sugar content (20.2 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and its saturated fat content (9.5 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness, compounding cardiovascular risk beyond the moderate sodium level of 170 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 20.2 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate 35.6 g/100 g with only 1.5 g/100 g fibre produces a high glycaemic load from refined flour sponge and sweetened cream filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Swiss roll as a routine dessert because its sugar content of 20.2 g/100 g and estimated GI of 65 produce a glycaemic load of approximately 13.9 per 60 g serving, causing significant post-meal glucose excursions; if consumed on special occasions, limit to one thin slice and pair with a protein source to blunt the glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 9.5 g/100 g and trans fat 0.2 g/100 g from butter and cream raise LDL cholesterol and promote endothelial inflammation; high sugar further contributes to dyslipidaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid Swiss roll because its saturated fat content of 9.5 g/100 g and trace trans fat of 0.2 g/100 g raise LDL cholesterol and promote vascular inflammation, while the high sugar load of 20.2 g/100 g contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial dysfunction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 9.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; trans fat 0.2 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory; sugar 20.2 g/100 g promotes inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Swiss roll because its saturated fat (9.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from butter and cream upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and its high sugar content (20.2 g/100 g) further promotes systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Swiss roll is a classic Western baked dessert consisting of a thin, light sponge cake sheet that is spread with a sweetened cream filling and tightly rolled into a cylindrical log shape before slicing. The sponge is made from wheat flour, eggs, sugar, and butter, producing a soft, moist, and slightly springy texture. The cream filling — typically whipped cream or buttercream — adds richness and sweetness. Per 100 g, Swiss roll provides 361 kcal, 7.3 g protein, 21.1 g fat (of which approximately 9.5 g is saturated fat and 0.2 g is trans fat), 35.6 g carbohydrate, 20.2 g sugar, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 170 mg sodium. The high sugar content (20.2 g/100 g) and low fibre (1.5 g/100 g) make it unsuitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease as a regular food. The estimated glycaemic index of 65 (medium) and a glycaemic load of approximately 13.9 per 60 g serving reflect its rapid glucose-raising potential. Swiss roll is widely consumed as a snack or dessert across Singapore, Southeast Asia, and globally, and is available in numerous flavour variants including pandan, chocolate, and strawberry. It is vegetarian-friendly but contains gluten, dairy, and eggs, making it unsuitable for those with these allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of Swiss roll?
Is Swiss roll high in sugar?
What is the GI of Swiss roll?
Is Swiss roll suitable for hypertension?
What are the ingredients in Swiss roll?
How much fat is in Swiss roll?
Is Swiss roll the same as a jelly roll?

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02010018, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). The source is an approved primary regional source. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (plain sponge cake, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) and clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g (9.5 g/100 g) and trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) are estimated from typical Swiss roll composition (butter and cream filling) as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated and trans fat for this entry; these values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for similar cream-filled sponge cake products and are flagged as estimated. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Swiss roll entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or separately available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026. 3. Swiss roll is a Western/international dessert and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the G6 multilingual rule, which applies to Singaporean dishes. No mandatory local-language names are required for this food category. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on plain sponge cake (GI 66) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for Swiss roll exists in approved sources. Glycaemic load per 60 g serving = (65 × 35.6 × 0.60) / 100 = 13.9 g.
